title: Towards Practical Defect-Focused Automated Code Review

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a defect-focused, merge-request–level automated code review framework that integrates static code slicing, a multi-role LLM workflow (reviewer/meta-reviewer/validator/translator), a comment filtering mechanism aimed at reducing nitpicks and hallucinations, and a line-localization prompt design. Evaluated on reconstructed real-world failures from an industrial C++ codebase and integrated into a DevOps pipeline, the approach reports substantially higher key-bug inclusion and composite performance versus prior snippet-level baselines, with ablations attributing gains to flow-based slicing, multi-role aggregation, and filtering.
strengths: Elevates automated code review from snippet-level code-to-text toward repository- and MR-level analysis centered on defect detection rather than text similarity.
Introduces a practical combination of AST-guided code slicing (Left Flow/Full Flow), multi-role LLM collaboration, and line-aware prompting targeted at real developer workflows.
Proposes Key Bug Inclusion (KBI) and Comprehensive Performance Index (CPI) as MR-level metrics better aligned with real-world defect detection than BLEU/ROUGE.

Uses real incident-driven MRs and postmortem/fix information rather than synthetic defects; includes multiple LLM backends and detailed ablation across slicing modes, reviewer counts, validation stages, and line-number encodings.
Reports behavior of trade-offs (e.g., validator reduces FAR but can reduce recall), and shows heterogeneity of slicing strategies via a Venn analysis.
The end-to-end pipeline and roles are clearly depicted; motivations, challenges, and high-level designs are well articulated.
Empirical sections answer well-posed RQs with structured tables and concise summaries per RQ.
Addresses a highly practical problem with strong industrial relevance; the metrics and line-aware comment injection reflect real deployment considerations.
The findings (e.g., shorter but targeted context via Left Flow outperforming longer Full Flow) are actionable for practitioners and future research.
weaknesses: Heavy reliance on heuristic scoring (Q1–Q3, 1–7) and fixed thresholds introduces subjectivity; validator stages sometimes harm KBI substantially, suggesting instability and limited robustness.

Limited methodological detail on slicing algorithms (e.g., precise data/control dependence construction, interprocedural scope, aliasing, templates in C++) hinders reproducibility and transferability.
The multi-role architecture mostly orchestrates existing LLM capabilities; the core algorithmic novelty (beyond careful system design) is limited for an ML venue.

Baseline comparisons appear mismatched: snippet-level T5-style models are assessed at MR-level with filters they were not designed for; no adaptation or fair MR-level orchestration is provided, which may exaggerate the performance gap.

Missing comparisons to strong non-LLM/static-analysis baselines (e.g., Clang-Tidy, Infer, CodeQL, Cppcheck) that are widely used for bug finding; omitting them weakens claims about practical defect detection.
Dataset details (sample size, bug categories, MR sizes) are mainly in appendices; no inter-annotator agreement or quality controls are reported for ground-truth KBI mapping.
No statistical significance tests or confidence intervals for key metrics; some runs average over three trials only for a subset (single reviewer), limiting conclusions about variance.
FAR remains very high (often >75–90%), casting doubt on deployability without strong human triage; real-world A/B or user-acceptance studies are not reported.
Resource and latency budgets are not discussed; using very large LLMs for all roles may be impractical at scale.
Contradictory statements about using only open-source, locally deployable models while including clearly closed or API-based models (e.g., Mistral-large, Command R+) reduce clarity and credibility.
Definitions of KBI, CPI, and LSR are deferred to appendices; main text would benefit from concise formal definitions and concrete examples.
LSR measures “valid lines” rather than correctness in some settings, which can overstate localization quality.
Under-cites or does not empirically compare against established static analyzers/SAST tools and code query systems that are standard practice in industry.
Limited discussion of retrieval/RAG-style program analysis for LLMs (tree-sitter–based retrieval, callgraph/dataflow–driven RAG) and multi-agent LLM frameworks specifically for code tasks.
reviews.questions:
1. What is the exact size and composition of the incident-derived MR dataset (number of MRs, repositories, average LOC/touch per MR, bug categories/severity)? Please provide main-text statistics and any inter-annotator agreement for KBI ground truth.
2. How is KBI computed in practice? What constitutes “inclusion” of a key bug (lexical overlap, semantic match, human adjudication)? How do you ensure consistent, objective scoring across cases?
3. How were snippet-level baselines adapted to the MR-level KBI/FAR setting? Could you provide results where baselines are equipped with the same slicing, aggregation, and line-localization wrappers to control for orchestration effects?
4. Why were static analyzers and SAST tools (e.g., Clang-Tidy, Infer, CodeQL, Cppcheck) not included as baselines? Can you report their KBI/FAR on your dataset to contextualize LLM-based gains?
5. The paper states only open-source and locally deployable models were used, yet lists models like Mistral-large and Command R+ that are not open. Please clarify model availability, deployment mode, and how this affects reproducibility and the stated security constraints.
6. What is the runtime, token usage, and compute cost per MR for typical configurations (e.g., Left Flow/Full Flow, 3 reviewers + validator)? How does heterogeneous model assignment change cost-effectiveness?
7. Can you report statistical significance (e.g., bootstrap CIs) for the main comparisons in Table 2 and ablations? Some deltas appear small relative to likely variance.
8. How sensitive are results to the Q1–Q3 thresholds? Have you explored learned or adaptive filters (e.g., supervised calibration on reviewer accept/reject labels) and can you share early findings?
9. For LSR, can you report “correct-line” localization rates (not just valid format) and downstream impact on developer actionability (e.g., time-to-fix, acceptance rate) from internal pilots?
10. Beyond C++, how portable is the slicing (e.g., Java/Go/Rust)? What engineering effort is needed to replicate Left/Full Flow in other ecosystems?
reviews.assessment: This work tackles an important, under-explored gap by reframing automated code review around MR-level defect detection and actual developer workflows. The system contributions—slicing, multi-role orchestration, filtering, and line localization—are thoughtfully engineered and supported by ablations and real incident data. However, the evaluation has substantial methodological gaps: baseline fairness is questionable (snippet-level models assessed in an MR-level pipeline), static-analysis baselines are missing, dataset/annotation transparency is limited, and FAR remains very high, raising concerns about deployability. Algorithmic novelty is moderate, and many details critical for reproducibility (slicing specifics, metrics, thresholds, resource usage) are deferred or incomplete. Overall, this is a promising and practically relevant system paper with clear insights, but it currently falls short of ICML standards for methodological rigor and reproducibility. Strengthening baselines (including static analyzers), clarifying dataset/metrics, adding statistical rigor and cost analyses, and demonstrating improved FAR or real user acceptance would make this substantially more compelling.
